expectations.evidence: Response compares core differences throughout: relational vs document model (lines 9-10), transaction support (lines 13-19), query complexity (lines 24-25), and includes a dedicated section 'When MongoDB Makes Sense' (lines 31-38) contrasting use cases.

expectations.text: 分析了扩展性(10k到100k订单/天)的影响
expectations.evidence: Lines 27-28 directly address scaling: '10k-100k orders/day is well within PostgreSQL's capabilities with proper indexing and standard optimization. Many companies handle millions of transactions daily on PostgreSQL.'

expectations.text: 讨论了数据模型灵活性vs可靠性的权衡
expectations.evidence: Lines 21-22 discuss flexibility: 'The "flexibility" argument for MongoDB is oversold here. PostgreSQL supports JSONB columns if you need schema flexibility.' Lines 42-43 address reliability: 'PostgreSQL has decades of battle-testing. Data corruption is extremely rare. MongoDB has improved but had historical issues with data loss.'

expectations.text: 针对电商场景(products/orders/users/inventory)给出具体建议
expectations.evidence: Lines 9-10 specifically reference e-commerce entities: 'orders reference users and products, inventory links to products, order items connect orders to products.' Lines 14-17 provide concrete e-commerce transaction example with order creation, inventory updates, payment processing, and user history.

expectations.text: 提供了明确的推荐和理由
expectations.evidence: Clear recommendation at line 3: 'Recommendation: PostgreSQL' followed by line 5 'For your e-commerce platform, PostgreSQL is the better choice.' Lines 50-52 provide bottom line: 'Go with PostgreSQL. It matches your use case better, reduces complexity, and gives you stronger guarantees around data integrity and transactions—critical for e-commerce.'

claims.claim: PostgreSQL has rock-solid transaction support while MongoDB's multi-document transactions are more limited
claims.evidence: Accurate - PostgreSQL has mature ACID transaction support, MongoDB added multi-document transactions in version 4.0 with known limitations

claims.claim: MongoDB had historical issues with data loss
claims.evidence: Accurate - MongoDB had well-documented data loss issues in earlier versions (pre-3.0) related to journaling and replication

claims.claim: 10k-100k orders/day is well within PostgreSQL's capabilities
claims.evidence: Accurate - this represents roughly 1-12 orders per second, easily handled by properly configured PostgreSQL

eval_feedback.overall: No suggestions, evals look solid. All assertions test meaningful outcomes that require substantive analysis of the database choice problem.
